speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Reverend Ernest Levy, and this is tape number two. You spoke about your arrival in Hungary. Could you say what happened to your family, and how you reassembled in Budapest, and your life, which was the beginning of the war, but in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our [NON-ENGLISH], in Hungary, was not the best one. Our welcome was, I would say, terrible. We came out of central station, walked along the ring road, and the first thing we realized, I was with my late father. He was holding my hand. There was a demonstration, anti-Jewish demonstration, about 500 people, shouting slogans, anti-Jewish slogans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine that? To arrive from the-- from trouble, into-- into more trouble? And that was the [NON-ENGLISH]. And we knew [LAUGHS] we didn't-- we didn't arrive into paradise. So eventually, we had to start life from zero. So in the evening, I went to school. My Hungarian wasn't good enough for school yet, right? So I had to learn the language properly. And in the evening, we went to [NON-ENGLISH], which was a special school for-- Boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: --boys. And during the day, I had to earn, somebody had to earn money. We were penniless, homeless, penniless refugees. And Budapest had a large Jewish population. And they had to learn still how to organize themselves to look after refugees. They didn't-- had no-- no-- no experience in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, certainly, doors have opened. And I started eating everyday in a different Jewish home, little boy of 14. Can you imagine that? Every day in a different home? And I developed in no time at all, stomach ulcers. I wasn't used to all these different Hungarian cooking and food. You had to eat what you got.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the nervousness, the tension to live away. I was really brought up very sheltered. And suddenly we had to face the music. The world was terribly difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the family-- the oldest brother, started teaching English to earn a few pounds, right? And so father started-- tried to start some-- some little business to earn a few pounds. But it was terribly difficult. I remember, I earned three pounds a week in a little watch shop, where I learned engineering, light engineering. three pounds a week. And my late mother was waiting for the three pounds on Friday, so that we had food for Shabbat. Be the youngest had to earn to keep the family going.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on Shabbat, the family came together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came together, lived in one large room in Budapest, the whole family. [NON-ENGLISH] Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did your parents make connections with the community, and go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Again, my singing helped an awful lot. Whenever we appeared, when I opened my mouth and I sang, we conquered. We were accepted. The doors opened wide. And really, that was a great, great, wonderful advantage. My singing, my performances. Eventually, father took me to the big shul. He wanted me to join a choir, because the choir were paid. The choirs were paid in those times. But the choir master said, [INAUDIBLE] no more singing. The boy's 14 years old. His voice is breaking. No, I'm not taking him. And he said to my father, Mr. Lowy, don't let the boy carry on the singing. He will have beautiful voice, but stop the singing now. And [NON-ENGLISH] we stopped the singing. I had to sing another year, and sing and perform in Moishele. My name is Moishe, as well. Moishe, please. I had-- father, I can't, I can't. Please, Moishe, just a little. And so I sang for another year. It was murder, really, you know, when your voice breaks. And still to this day, I suffer from abusing my voice when I should have listened to the choir master, and no more singing. I have problems with my singing. Although, let's see, I have success as well. But I feel it. That that year, when father forced me to perform and to sing-- which is understandable-- did lot of harm to my voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these were the years in Budapest. Then I changed, because that was too heavy for me, that job. So father put me in a-- in a fountain pen factory. A small factory. It's an orthodox Jew running it, right? and so I had Shabbat, [NON-ENGLISH] off, no problems. And that where I stayed till the end, till-- till 1944. And did your-- during those years, did your family stay together from a orthodox point of view?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In the meantime, two sisters got married, you see. And one brother got a job in the provinces, in Hungary. And so there was-- the family was smaller.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those years, '39 to '44, what was life like for the Jewish population of Hungary who'd been there before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad at all. Although, from 1941, it's already thousands of Jewish men have been taken to so-called labor camps, Hungarian labor camps. Which are called, [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian. [HUNGARIAN]. Labor camps. Still, the-- the conditions were fairly humane, not too bad. But it became worse, and worse, and worse. I remember davening in the Polish shul, in Budapest. How they came, men, to the rabbi, to the Munkacz Rebbes, the young Muncacz Rebbes, he should bless them because they've got to go to the labor camp. And they kissed his hands, and they blessed him, [NON-ENGLISH], blah, blah, blah. And that I witnessed every morning in shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was from 1941, '42, they-- they were taken to labor camp. But generally, in-- in Hungary-- I mean, Jews were thrown out from-- from concert platforms. Jews were throwing-- thrown out from the opera, from the universities, right. They've had the numerous [NON-ENGLISH] going. But all in all, it was not too bad, Hungary, till the invasion of Hungary by the Germans. Yes. But, the labor camps, were they organized by Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there antisemitism in that, or was it mainly a question of finding work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Antisemitism. It was very, very hard. Antisemitic abuse, you got from your superiors non-stop. And as time went on, it became worse, and worse, and worse. It was a nightmare. In the end, it was a nightmare to be called up to the labor camps. Many did not return.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of your family went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother Charles had to go to labor camps-- labor camp. My brother Miklosh-- Max, went to a labor camp, yes. Max did never return. Eventually he returned, in 1946. But he was-- he was announced missing, and he was taken to Moscow. He was-- he was-- he fell as a prisoner of war to the Russians, eventually, and was taken to Moscow and brainwashed. And he came back in 1946. We thought he is dead. That was fantastic. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were between the age of 14 and 19 during your stay in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the Munkacz Rebbes, famous rabbi, did he speak about the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Officially?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must tell. He was an absolute brilliant brain, and brilliant man. He didn't like this hocus pocus business, kissing his hand. But he couldn't help it. They really worshipped him. And I remember, in 1943, end of 1943, again, in the late autumn, there was a Shabbat afternoon. A [NON-ENGLISH], as we called it, where he always spoke. On to that [NON-ENGLISH], it became darker, and darker, and darker, for a full house, full house in the shul. They sat around the big table for [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was going through the Munkacz, it was a young Munkacz, Rabinovich. He was going, and didn't say a word. And became darker. And we didn't have [NON-ENGLISH] yet. And then a most frightening thing happened. He actually called God to the [NON-ENGLISH]. I've never experienced like that. We were sitting with my father and brother, Alexander, in the seat. In the pew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain [NON-ENGLISH]. Could you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] he was calling God to court, if that's the right translation. And he was pleading, how long can you wait? It's now the time to help! Please! How can we believe in you? How can we? Come, help! Do something! Show yourself! It was so frightening. It was like, I couldn't-- shaking like that. It was terrible. Frightening. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- against his will, he was taken out of Budapest. Against his will. I must say that. He didn't want leave. But he was taken out in 1944, in the early months of 1944. He was taken out of Budapest, and he survived the war. He became the chief rabbi of, I think, of South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those years, did news infiltrate about the worst aspects of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They did not know about the death camps. We did not know. Truly, we did not know what was going on really, in Poland and Germany. We did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Hungarians fought with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although it was an independent country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an independent country. They fought with the Germans, yes. And I think they committed the greatest crime of World War II, the Hungarians. I consider that the greatest crime, what the Hungarians committed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fighting with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Fighting with the Germans, all right. But to start, the killing in 1944, when everybody, it was obvious the war was lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what happened in 1944, when the situation in Hungary changed? What happened? Can you recall it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? [NON-ENGLISH], relatively speaking, a normal human being, was trying to hold the reigns. But for a one day kingship. That fascist, idiotic leader, Szalasi, took over. And five minutes before the end of the war, 600,000 Hungarian people, Jewish people, perished in the-- in different concentration camps. The last minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of saying, stop it! Can't you see? No. The last-- killing to the last minute. I consider that the greatest crime. Thousands of people were shot into in Danube, innocent people. That was after the invasion by Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not even-- that was after the invasion, yes. Because the invasion was the 19th of January, 1944, remember. After the invasion, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the 19th of January, '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Very well. I remember coming out of a cinema with my brother, Alexander. And there was no traffic, no trams going. The-- the city was paralyzed, dead. And everywhere, the German soldiers with a machine gun, spread out legs, standing. That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're sitting in the cinema for two hours. We came out, and that was it, facing us. It was frightening. I don't have to tell you. How do we get home at all? But certainly we got home, but from there on-- [SIGHS] Did you realize that the German army had invaded that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We realized. We realized. We realized. Because they were-- they were everywhere. The hotel entrance, German soldiers. Every public building, German soldiers. Everywhere you, looked, German soldiers in the helmet. They usually, you know, with the machine gun, straight out, legs, and the boots, the usual picture of the German soldiers standing there, arrogantly, right? The-- the occupier.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family the days after this invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a problem. I did not attend the pre-military [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] training, which was compulsory. I didn't attend it, not for a year, for two years. Partly because I hated it, because it was terrible. The antisemitic abuse we got from our superiors. It was too terrible for worth what they did to humiliate us. But partly because my boss just didn't let me go also. And I didn't mind. And there were two years went by without me attending that. That was a great crime. So eventually, I got four days imprisonment.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the invasion. Four days imprisonment, which means community work, more or less, for the imprisonment. And so we didn't know what to do. That was hanging over us these four days. So we walked into the police station with my late father. And I say, look, I want to get over these four days, right? And the rest-- father went home, I stayed in the police station, and was taken to another prison, and got mixed up with the first transport to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days after the invasion was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The invasion was in 19th of March, And I-- that happened, my voluntary walking into trouble, happened on the 13th of April. Did

speaker: interviewer
text: You have contact with your family during the few days-- or let's say the few weeks you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever. I was already in Auschwitz when they still thought I'm in the-- in the police station, or in somewhere in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what happened when you were sent to Auschwitz? How did this take place? Were you with Hungarian police at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian mounted police were herding us towards the central station in Budapest, about 2,000 people, remember. Screaming, shouting, hitting us. And behind the column of people were the Germans in their Mercedes cars, following. And they wanted to impress. They wanted to impress the Germans, and they're screaming and they're shouting. Eventually we arrive in the central station. In no time at all, I found myself in the wagon with about 80 other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came from the police cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the police cell into a-- into a proper prison. Because the policeman said, oh, you can't stay here. Because in the police-- in the police prison, you can only stay for two days. Anything over two days, you have to go to the-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ended up with the [NON-ENGLISH] prison, where on Friday morning, I got mixed up with the Jewish crowd who was already ready. Where did the Jewish crowd come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from the provinces. They suffered first. The provinces suffered first. They came from the provinces.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a mass deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the provinces first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I wanted to eat kosher. Otherwise, I would have been taken for my-- for my community work. But I wanted to eat kosher. I said, and they told me, if you want eat kosher, here are the Jews. They get wonderful food from the Jewish community. And so I got into the big place where there were thousands already. Never could-- never managed to get out. And so instead serving four days of community work, I ended up in Auschwitz with these unfortunate people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're now on the 23rd of April, when you were put in the truck. Could you speak about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Put in the truck, in Budapest, or in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Budapest

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest? The most tragic part of it was, you couldn't relieve yourself. That was the most tragic. How do you do that? People have found all kinds of tricks. I could not. I was too inhibited. And so I suffered many months from that. Because my arrival in Auschwitz did not help. I just could not relieve myself. That was a major problem, apart from the lack of water or food. But in one day, we made the journey in one day. So that wasn't so tragic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the arrival was beyond description. The arrival in Auschwitz, no writer, no movie, no nobody can describe, ever, an arrival of thousands of people thrown out from the wagons. Where they were received by the vicious dogs there, with the SS, right? And families torn apart, babies taken away, and children, and the screaming. I mean the tragedy is indescribable on this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was single. I was on my own. And I looked around. I'm a very good observer. And I saw every individual tragedy. A father kneeling down and screaming to him, my daughter, give me my daughter! Give me back my daughter, Eva! Give me back my daughter, Eva! Please! I mean that was just-- you have to multiply that in-- in-- in thousands. Indescribable. And the Germans arrived in there, elegant. Cars, right, and Mengele was going up and down, and not even taking any notice of that enormous tragedy and catastrophe. Nothing. So 500 went to that side, 1500 went to the right side. Came out, we were marched off. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Dr-- did you know it was Dr. Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Just in retrospect, you know. But how could I have ever known? But I remember him quite well. Good looking man. Good looking, I remember. They were joking, and laughing between themselves. I remember that so well. Ha, ha, ha! You know. And going up and down. As if nothing would-- if that would be the most natural thing in the world, that thousands of innocent, unarmed, wonderful people, citizens, of your own country, are thrown into that-- into that-- into that madness, into that hands of mad men.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was 25th of April?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, more or less, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We left on-- we left on the 15th of April. No. We left on the 13th. We arrived on the 15th of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: 15th of April?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said before, 23rd, but it should've been 15th?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, these dates-- these dates are not 100% accurate.

speaker: interviewer
text: This arrival in Auschwitz, with-- did Dr. Mengele-- was he in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a commander, the top man. The top man. He was actually looking for-- for-- for-- to select certain people, which he would use for his experiments-- medical experiments. He was looking for that. But by and large, it went quickly, very quickly. The screaming, the shouting, the begging, the whimpering, did not effect him at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: In uniform, oh, but how. Sparkling uniform, right? Oh, my God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the 500 were marched into the camp. And our first experience was terrible. A Polish prisoner sneaked over to us because he was asking for our watches. Because he knew we are going to lose everything. And a kapo got hold of him and killed him there on the spot, in front of me. In front of me, just down there, killed him, there and then. Imagine the welcome to Auschwitz? Where you arrived in a world of evilness, beyond any description.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say what a kapo is, and how that arose?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapo, very often, were people released from prisons, remember. Criminals who were-- got free hand from the Germans. Here, you're in charge. Imagine that? Criminals-- to put criminals in charge of decent, normal, lovely people, wonderful people. Imagine that? And certainly there were people who were made into kapos, because they proved to be the type. You have to be the type, for survival you did anything to survive. For another plate of soup, you did anything. Eventually they were picked out. And these were the people who were more-- more-- more beasts than people. Worse than the Germans, to impress.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Polish and Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish and Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this kapo who killed the man, what was the reason for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was not allowed to make any contact with the new prisoners, with the new group of prisoners. He was not allowed. It was like nothing for him, nothing. It was absolutely the most natural thing that he killed him there, nothing. And I remember, he got hold of his, whatever it was, his jacket, pulled him away. Bang. That's it, finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he shot him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No! Killed him. Hacked him to death, there in front of me. I mean, we didn't eat a lot. These people, they're already there for years, remember. And they were not the strongest, fittest anymore. So they didn't eat a lot. But he hacked him to death there in front of me. In front of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were other people seeing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all looking, looking on. We were all looking on. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would not know. You remember, once you are in Auschwitz, you had no name. You had a number. If you weren't tattooed, you had a little metal plate in your-- on your-- hanging around your neck, and that was your number. You're no name. Name was finished. You were no human being anymore. You just a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue what happened after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [SIGHS] it's very difficult to describe the misery of the few hours in Auschwitz. The humiliation, the degradation [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of people. Impossible to describe. So we were led in to get shaved by blunt instruments, in seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the 500 selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 500. Yes, you had to stand on a stool, and the kapos were doing the job, Polish kapos, and Polish prisoners. And it went-- in no time at all, you had dried. And they were bleeding from-- from everywhere. You were bleeding from everywhere, it was bleeding everywhere. Because it went so quickly. And the instruments with which you were shaved, they're so blunt, doing so quick. Imagine that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were bleeding from everywhere. I was 19 years old, finding myself with a world of that evilness. Imagine that? I couldn't believe it, what's going on? Everything went so quickly. And then you had to go through a arch, where there was a large, big fellow, kapo, standing with a pail and a sponge, big sponge, and giving over you, with that, disinfection stuff. And that went in into your-- into your-- into your wounds, everywhere into your rectum, everywhere. It was-- It was terrible. And then you were pushed into a shower. Thousands of hundreds of people into a shower, right? And I had a laughing fit. I had a laughing fit. It was so tragic, I had a laughing fit. I was on my own, right? I had a laughing fit.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were jumping like apes from the pain. I can't describe. It was comic, tragic comic. And then the hot shower came down, but boiling hot. There was nowhere to go away from that. It was-- that was the first hours in Auschwitz. And then, exhausted, dehumanized, you found yourself in a block, in Birkenau. I was taken to Birkenau. Later I got to know it was Birkenau--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was part of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Totally exhausted. Totally. And still, no relief for me. Totally exhausted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have things to drink at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No drink, no food. Nothing.
